coredata.dc:title: Activating the room-temperature phosphorescence of carbon dots for the dual-signal detection of tetracycline and information encryption
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jiankang
Feng, Zhiying
Zhou, Sen
Zeng, Linggao
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) attract the numerous explorations owing to their promising prospects in multiple fields, howbeit, their phosphorescence in aqueous barely lasts for long due to the quenching effects originated from the dissolved oxygen, and thus it is of a great challenge to acquire the water-soluble phosphorescent CDs. We here proposed one kind of solid-state RTP CDs through a microwave strategy using tetraethylenepentamine and phosphoric acid as the precursors. Significantly, we further employed tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) as the matrix, which could encapsulate the previous CDs, thus facilitating the formation of the compact structure and activating their long-lived and high-efficiency phosphorescence in aqueous. On the basis of their fluorescence and phosphorescence, a dual-signal strategy of detecting tetracycline by CDs@TEOS was successfully established, and this detection exhibited a fluorescent linear-range of 2 nM to 90 μM as well as a phosphorescent linear-range of 30 nM to 300 μM towards assaying tetracycline, broadening the dual-signal ways of assaying tetracycline. Additionally, the CDs prepared here showed the great potential of serving as the RTP ink for the information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aqueous room-temperature phosphorescence
Dual-signal detection
Tetracycline
Detection mechanism

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Investigation of the selectivity and potential interference
Detection of tetracycline in actual samples
Preparation and the optical property of CDs
Fluorescent and phosphorescent stability of CDs
Activating CDs to emit the aqueous phosphorescence
The optical property and characterization of CDs@TEOS
Fluorescence and phosphorescence stability of CDs@TEOS
Selectivity and potential interference for CDs@TEOS assaying tetracycline
Establishing the dual-signal assay of tetracycline
Detection of tetracycline in the pork sample
Mechanism of the fluorescent and phosphorescent response of CDs@TEOS to tetracycline

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China (cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0027, CSTB2022NSCQ-MSX1523), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( SWU-XDJH202321 ), SAMR Science and Technology Program (2022MK105), Chongqing Scientific Institution Incentive Performance Guiding Special Projects (cstc2022jxj120036),Performance Incentive and Guidance Special Project of Scientific Research Organization, Chongqing Science and Technology (cstc2021jxjl130024).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of synthesizing CDs and CDs@TEOs and the dual-channel detection of tetracycline.

(A) Fluorescence spectrum of CDs (inset: CDs with (a) daylight and (b) UV); (B) Phosphorescence spectrum of CDs loaded on the paper (inset: CDs with (c) UV on and (d) UV off); (C) Fluorescence and (D) phosphorescence spectra of CDs with different excitations; Patterns of epidemic prevention worker (E), branch (F), mascot (G), dove of peace (H) painted by CDs with daylight, UV on or UV off.

(A) HR-TEM image of CDs (inset: DLS result of CDs); (B) FTIR spectrum and (C) XPS survey of CDs; High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s (D), N 1 s (E), O 1 s (F) and P 2p (G) for CDs; (H) UV–vis spectrum of CDs; (I) XRD pattern of CDs.

(A) Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of CDs@TEOS (inset: CDs@TEOS with (a) UV on and (b) UV off); (B) Fluorescence and (C) phosphorescence spectra of CDs@TEOS with different excitations; (D) Images recorded by different time for the phosphorescence of CDs@TEOS; (E) HR-TEM image of CDs@TEOS; (F) FTIR spectrum of CDs@TEOS.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs@TEOS without or with TC (inset: images of CDs@TEOS (a) and CDs@TEOS with TC (b) with UV on); (D) Phosphorescence spectra of CDs@TEOS without or with TC (inset: images of CDs@TEOS without (a) or with TC (b) with UV off); (B) Fluorescence and (E) phosphorescence spectra of CDs@TEOS with varying concentrations of TC; Linear relationship of CDs@TEOS assaying TC through fluorescence (C) and phosphorescence (F).

(A) Zeta potentials of CDs@TEOS, TC and CDs@TEOS with TC; (B) FTIR spectra of CDs@TEOS without and with TC; (C) UV–vis spectrum of TC and phosphorescence spectrum of CDs@TEOS; (D) UV–vis spectrum of TC and fluorescence spectrum of CDs@TEOS; (E) Illustrating the mechanism for the quenched fluorescence and phosphorescence of CDs@TEOS by TC.

head.ce:title: Activating the room-temperature phosphorescence of carbon dots for the dual-signal detection of tetracycline and information encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Academy of Metrology and Quality Inspection, Chongqing 401121, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Academy of Metrology and Quality Inspection

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Academy of Metrology and Quality Inspection, Chongqing 401121, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: NMPA Key Laboratory for Quality Monitoring of Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances, Chongqing Institute for Food and Drug Control, Chongqing 401121, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: NMPA Key Laboratory for Quality Monitoring of Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances
Chongqing Institute for Food and Drug Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: NMPA Key Laboratory for Quality Monitoring of Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances, Chongqing Institute for Food and Drug Control, Chongqing 401121, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We here proposed one kind of solid room-temperature phosphorescent CDs using tetraethylenepentamine and phosphoric acid as the precursors, Subsequently, we encapsulated the carbon dots obtained using the matrix of tetraethoxysilane (CDs@TEOS), thus achieving the long-lived RTP in aqueous. Importantly, introducing tetracycline into CDs@TEOS decreased both their fluorescence and phosphorescence intensity, and thus we successfully established a dual-signal detecting method of tetracycline on the basis of this phenomenon. Meanwhile, we further employed the prepared CDs as the RTP ink to create various patterns.

Carbon dots (CDs) with room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) attract the numerous explorations owing to their promising prospects in multiple fields, howbeit, their phosphorescence in aqueous barely lasts for long due to the quenching effects originated from the dissolved oxygen, and thus it is of a great challenge to acquire the water-soluble phosphorescent CDs. We here proposed one kind of solid-state RTP CDs through a microwave strategy using tetraethylenepentamine and phosphoric acid as the precursors. Significantly, we further employed tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) as the matrix, which could encapsulate the previous CDs, thus facilitating the formation of the compact structure and activating their long-lived and high-efficiency phosphorescence in aqueous. On the basis of their fluorescence and phosphorescence, a dual-signal strategy of detecting tetracycline by CDs@TEOS was successfully established, and this detection exhibited a fluorescent linear-range of 2 nM to 90 μM as well as a phosphorescent linear-range of 30 nM to 300 μM towards assaying tetracycline, broadening the dual-signal ways of assaying tetracycline. Additionally, the CDs prepared here showed the great potential of serving as the RTP ink for the information encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, the carbon nanomaterials with more and more unique physical and chemical property have been discovered and elucidated [1]. Carbon dots, being a member of luminescent carbon nanomaterials, exhibit the great potential during the processes of biochemical analysis [2] and biological imaging [3,4], chemical catalysis [5–7] and environmental monitoring [8] by virtue of their unique structure and attractive optical performance [9–11]. Among various carbon dots, there recently appear one new kind of CDs, emitting not only the fluorescence but also the room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) [12–14]. These carbon dots, especially with the high-stability and long-life phosphorescence, show the particular advantages mainly including the dual signals towards various analytical purposes [15,16]. Howbeit, the triplet excitons prefer to be devitalized, thus leading to their short phosphorescence lifetime of most RTP CDs, and their phosphorescence are frequently quenched by the dissolved oxygen, thus the corresponding applications of these CDs are greatly restricted [17–21]. Thereby, developing the long-lived phosphorescence CDs in aqueous is meaningful but challengeable.........

Antibiotics, being widely employed to kill bacteria, play a crucial role in people’s daily life all around the world. However, abusing antibiotics leads to a series of problems such as imperiling the drinking water, contaminating the environment, threating human being’s health, raising the drug-resistance and beyond [22,23], thus several developed countries have introduced the policy to restrict the abusive use of antibiotics in livestock. As a classical member of antibiotics, tetracycline (TC) is widely utilized in the fields of agriculture and animal husbandry until now, especially serving as the veterinary antibiotic, since TC shows the high activity of anti-bacteria and favorable water solubility [24,25]. Unfortunately, TC is hard to metabolize, and the undesirable accumulation may lead to the teratogenicity, especially lots of people have suffered from the tetracycline teeth [26]. Thus, kinds of contributions have been raised for the residue detection of TC mainly such as highly performance liquid chromatography [27] and mass spectrometry [28], and there scarcely exist the dual- signal methods for assaying TC residue.

In this study, we originally established a strategy of preparing the carbon dots by using a microwave-assisted method, while tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) and phosphoric acid served as the precursors. Subsequently, we encapsulated the carbon dots obtained here using the matrix of tetraethoxysilane (CDs@TEOS), thus achieving the long-lived room-temperature phosphorescence in aqueous. To be specific, CDs@TEOS exhibited both the blue fluorescence and long-lived green RTP, which could be visually observed for up to 10 s. Importantly, introducing tetracycline into CDs@TEOS reduced both their fluorescence and phosphorescence intensity, and thus we successfully established a dual-signal detecting method of tetracycline on the basis of this phenomenon, which exhibited the fluorescent detection limit of 1.8 nM as well as the phosphorescent detection limit of 10.3 nM. Meanwhile, we further employed the prepared CDs as the RTP ink to create various patterns, proving their potential applications towards information encryption (Fig.1).

Riboflavin (VB2), glucose, fructose, phosphoric acid, creatine, potassium bromide, manganese chloride (MnCl2), strontium chloride (SrCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), silver chloride (AgCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), lead chloride (PbCl2), cupro chloride (CuCl2), acetonitrile, ethanol, dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), methanol, tetrahydrofuran, sodium hydroxide were purchased from Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Tetracycline (TC) and clindamycin hydrochloride (CAS) were obtained from Xiensi Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, Chian). Also, D-histidine (D-His), L-cysteine (L-Cys), glutathione (GSH) and ammonia were acquired from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co.,Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Similarly, erythromycin (EM) was purchased from Bide Pharmatech Co.,Ltd. (Shanghai, China) as well as gentamicin sulfate (GM) from Shanghai yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Again, tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) was purchased from Meryer Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), while hydrochloric acid was from Chongqing Chuandong Chemical Group Co., Ltd (Chongqin, Chian). And we purchased tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) from Merda Technology Co., Ltd.(American). All these commercial reagents were of analytical grade and directly utilized without the further purification.

The fluorescent and phosphorescent spectra were performed by using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), and the UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained through a spectrophotometer of Shimadzu UV-2450 (Tokyo, Japan). The images of high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) were acquired by the microscope of TECNAI G2 F20 (Columbia, America) at 200 kV, while the size distribution was explored through the dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument of Zeta-sizer Nano (Malvern, England). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were provided with a Rigaku Ministar instrument (Tokyo, Japan). Similarly, ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (New York, America) and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Tokyo, Japan) were employed to explore the elemental compositions and functional groups of CDs and CDs@TEOS. Again, the microwave oven of Galanz (Shunde, China) was introduced to perform the syntheses, while the pH values of different solutions were detected with a digital pH meter of Fangzhong pHS-3C (Chengdu, China). All the separations were achieved by the centrifuge (TG16-W) of Cence instrument Co., Ltd. (Changsha, China).

Basically, 1 mL of TEPA was first dissolved with 5 mL of ultrapure water and was mixed with 1.2 mL of H3PO4 solution followed by continuously stirring for 2 min, and then this mixture was transferred into the microwave oven for 75 s at 550 W. After being cooled to room temperature, the crude product was dissolved with 10 mL of ultrapure water followed by adding 2 M of NaOH dropwise until obtaining the neutral pH. The obtained solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min and was subjected to the filtration by using the microporous membrane of 0.22 μm, and the supernatant was collected.

50 μL of CDs solution was initially diluted to 1.0 mL, while 1.0 mL of TEOS dispersed in 15 mL of ultrapure water, and the two solutions were adequately mixed. Then, 0.5 mL of ammonia was added to the previous solution followed by stirring at 25 °C for 8 h. The reaction product was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min, and was filtered with a 0.22 μm of microporous filter membrane to acquire a colorless transparent solution, and thus CDs@TEOS with the aqueous phosphorescence was obtained.

As a standard procedure, 200 μL of TC solutions with various concentrations (2 nM to 90 μM for fluorescence, 30 nM to 300 μM for phosphorescence) were introduced into the paralleled 200 μL of CDs@TEOS in multiple Eppendorf (EP) tubes of 1.5 mL, and these mixtures were incubated at 40 °C for 5 min. When the reactions finished, their fluorescence and phosphorescence intensity was separately measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the selectivity and potential interference
ce:section.ce:para: During the studying of the selectivity for CDS@TEOS detecting TC, 200 μL of CDs@TEOS was added to the multiple EP tubes of 1.5 mL, and 200 μL of various substances including TC erythromycin (EM), gentamicin sulfate (GM), clindamycin hydrochloride (CAS), D-histidine (D-His), L-cysteine (L-Cys), glutathione (GSH), riboflavin (VB2), creatine, fructose and glucose (each concentration for 10−4 M) was introduced into the previously paralleled tubes of CDs@TEOS, respectively. Accordingly, these mixtures were incubated at 40 °C for 5 min followed by the fluorescent and phosphorescent measurements. Towards investigating the potential interference originated from the coexisting substances, 200 μL of CDs@TEOS and 200 μL of TC at the concentration of 10−4 M were mixed into the paralleled EP tubes followed by introducing 200 μL of the single coexisting substance (each for 10−4 M), and their fluorescence and phosphorescence intensity were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of tetracycline in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: 5 g of churned pork, which was purchased from a local supermarket of Yonghui, was added into 20 mL of Na2EDTA-Mcllvaine buffer (0.1 M) followed by sonicating for 20 min. After the centrifugation, the supernatant was collected and subjected to the suction filtration, and the filtrate was further filtered with a 0.22 μm of microporous membrane. As a standard recovery test, the previously obtained supernatant was supplemented with different concentrations of TC, and the corresponding fluorescence and phosphorescence were measured, thus the recovery of TC was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and the optical property of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To acquire the favorable optical property of CDs, their synthesis conditions were initially optimized, where varied phosphorescent intensity was recorded by regulating the amounts of TEPA and phosphoric acid, reaction time. Accordingly, 1.0 mL of TEPA, 1.2 mL of H3PO4 and reaction time of 75 s were determined as the optimal conditions (Fig. S1). Subsequently, the optimal fluorescent excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs were identified as 416 nm and 476 nm through their fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 2 A). To achieve their solid-state RTP, the CDs were applied onto the filter paper, exhibiting the green RTP of 513 nm upon the excitation at 357 nm (Fig. 2B). The further findings described that the fluorescence emission of CDs changed accompanied by the excitation wavelengths rising from 376 nm to 466 nm (Fig. 2C), and different phosphorescent excitations within the range of 307 to 387 nm also resulted in their varied emissions (Fig. 2D), revealing that both their fluorescence and phosphorescence of CDs depended on the excitations.

To further understand the value of their fluorescence and phosphorescence, CDs were employed as the RTP ink for information encryption. To be specific, two patterns of epidemic prevention worker (Fig. 2E) and branch (Fig. 2F) were painted by the CDs prepared here, and both the patterns showed the green phosphorescence with UV off as well as the blue fluorescence with UV on. Similarly, the mascot of 2022 Winter Olympics (Fig. 2G) and dove of peace (Fig. 2H) were painted by using both CDs and a red fluorescent dye. Only the region coated with CDs exhibited the green phosphorescence with UV off, while the pattern drawn with the red fluorescent dye showed the red fluorescence with UV on. These findings demonstrated the potential of CDs serving as the RTP ink for information encryption.

To explore the surface morphology of CDs, HR-TEM was employed (Fig. 3 A), showing that CDs were spherical and well dispersed with the average size distribution of 4.13 nm, which was also confirmed by DLS analysis (Fig. 3A). Subsequently, FTIR was utilized to identify the functional groups of CDs. Primarily, the peak at 3355 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H (Fig. 3B), and the peaks at 2918 cm−1 corresponded to the bending vibration of C–H, while the peaks located at 1646, 1252, 1184 and 977 cm−1 were ascribed to the bending vibration of C = O, P = O, C-N and P-N, respectively. Again, the peak of 822 cm−1 was associated with the bending vibration of P-O-P, and the absorption at 524 cm−1 described the existence of PO4 3− [29,30].

Towards examining the element composition of CDs, their XPS survey was thereupon performed. The CDs were mainly contained four elements of C, N, O, and P, and their related percentages were 49.16 %, 13.24 %, 28.55 % and 9.05 % (Fig. 3C), respectively. For C 1 s, the curve was fitted into two peaks at 284.5 eV and 285.9 eV (Fig. 3D), which were originated from C-C/C-N and C-O. As shown in Fig. 3E, three characteristic peaks of N 1 s were attributed to C = N-C (399.6 eV), C-N/P-N (400.6 eV), N–H (401.4 eV). Meanwhile, the O 1 s survey was deconvoluted into three peaks at 530.4 eV, 531.7 eV and 532.3 eV, being ascribed to P = O, C-OH/C-O-C and P-O (Fig. 3F). Again, there appeared two characteristic peaks of P 2p (Fig. 3G), which were originated from P-O (132.9 eV) and P-N (133.7 eV) [29,31]. To further describe these CDs, their UV–vis spectrum with three peaks was recorded (Fig. 3H). In brief, the peak of 230 nm was attributed to the π-π* transition of C = C, while the two peaks of 290 nm and 350 nm were originated from the n-π* transition of C = O and C = N [30]. Additionally, the XRD pattern of CDs showed a peak at 25.7° (Fig. 3I), demonstrating the existence of disordered carbon atoms on their surfaces for CDs [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent and phosphorescent stability of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequently, we explored the optical stability of CDs with various conditions. In particular, the fluorescence intensity of CDs scarcely changed with varied time (Fig. S2A), temperature (Fig. S2B), pH (Fig. S2C), different concentrations of NaCl (Fig. S2D) and multiple metal ions (Fig. S2E). In contrast, several organic solvents except methanol showed the obvious effect on the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. S2F). These findings demonstrated that the CDs prepared here exhibited the generally favorable fluorescence. Similarly, we also evaluated the solid-state phosphorescence stability of CDs through testing the potential effect of pH, metal ions and organic solvents. The phosphorescence intensity of CDs was hardly affected by varying pH (Fig. S3A), and the metal ions (Fig. S3B) and organic solvents (Fig. S3C) also showed little negative effect, revealing their satisfactory phosphorescent stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activating CDs to emit the aqueous phosphorescence
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve emitting the phosphorescence in aqueous for CDs, TEOS and ammonia were introduced. During the reaction, TEOS decomposed to produce silica, which encapsulated the CDs through the formed covalent bonds. This encapsulation process could effectively decrease the non-radiative energy dissipation of CDs, and also prevent the negative effect of dissolved oxygen, thus achieving the aqueous phosphorescence of CDs [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical property and characterization of CDs@TEOS
ce:section.ce:para: By spectral scanning, the optimal fluorescence emission of CDs@TEOS was 400 nm with the excitation of 347 nm, while the optimal phosphorescence emission was 509 nm excitated at 353 nm (Fig. 4 A). The fluorescence emissions of CDs@TEOS varied with different excitation wavelengths between 257 nm and 417 nm (Fig. 4B), and various excitation wavelengths of 273 nm to 393 nm also resulted in the slightly changed phosphorescence emissions of CDs@TEOS (Fig. 4C). These results demonstrated that both the fluorescence and phosphorescence of CDs@TEOS depended on their excitations. Meanwhile, CDs@TEOS showed the bright green phosphorescence by the naked eye for up to 10 s with UV off, revealing that their phosphorescence was long-lived (Fig. 4D).

In addition, the morphology of CDs@TEOS was described by HR-TEM, and their particle size was larger than that of CDs (Fig. 4E), which was due to CDs was encapsulated by TEOS. Meanwhile, the functional groups of CDs@TEOS were identified by FTIR (Fig. 4F), and the absorption peak at 3356 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of N–H and O–H, the peak of 1652 cm−1 indicated the existence of C = O for CDs@TEOS. Again, the absorption peak at 1463 cm−1 was attributed the bending vibrations of C-Si, while the characteristic peaks of 1081 cm−1 and 810 cm−1 were separately originated from the stretching vibration of Si-O-Si and Si-N, while the peak at 521 cm−1 demonstrated the streching vibration of PO4 3− [33,34].

Taken together, the FTIR and XPS showed that CDs contained the functional groups including C = O, C = N, N-P and P = O, which acted as the subfluorophore for both FL and RTP, and XPS showed that the contents of N and P elements were 13.24 % and 9.05 %, respectively. The high-content doping elements of N and P facilitated ISC, thus effectively populating the triplet excitons. And FTIR of CDs@TEOS revealed the formation of C-Si and N-Si bonds, preventing the vibration and rotation of bonds. The introduction of TEOS created a rigid environment that inhibited the non-radiative transition and also avoided the phosphorescence quenching caused by dissolved oxygen. Thereby, the current CDs achieved the long-lived phosphorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and phosphorescence stability of CDs@TEOS
ce:section.ce:para: We further investigated the potential effect of different factors including pH, time of ultraviolet radiation and temperature on the fluorescence and phosphorescence of CDs@TEOS. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of CDs@TEOS was scarcely affected by varied pH (Fig. S4A), time of UV radiation (Fig. S4B) and temperature (Fig. S4C). Similarly, the time of ultraviolet radiation (Fig. S4E) and temperature (Fig. S4F) rather than pH (Fig. S4D) hardly affected the phosphorescence of CDs@TEOS. These findings demonstrated that the fluorescence and phosphorescence of CDs@TEOS were basically stable.

Considering the abundant groups on their surface, we thereby addressed whether CDs@TEOS could serve as the potential luminescent probe? Through screening several drugs, we found that both the fluorescence and phosphorescence of CDs@TEOS dramatically responded to TC (Fig. 5 A and 5B), suggesting that CDs@TEOS showed the possibility for establishing a dual- signal detection of TC. To obtain the optimal detection of TC by CDs@TEOS, we subsequently explored the conditions including detecting time and temperature, and thereby 5 min and 40 ℃ were selected (Figure S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and potential interference for CDs@TEOS assaying tetracycline
ce:section.ce:para: To further identify the selectivity of CDs@TEOS detecting TC, a series of related substance (EM, GM, CAS, D-His, L-Cys, GSH, Glucose, Fructose, VB2 and Creatine, each for 10−4 M) were investigated. The results demonstrated that both the fluorescence and phosphorescence intensity of CDs@TEOS with TC exhibited the obvious change compared with that by adding other substances (Fig. S6A and S6C), indicating their satisfactory selectivity during detecting TC. Meanwhile, we also tested the possible effect of different substances on CDs@TEOS assaying TC. And the results revealed that these substances scarcely affected the detection of TC by CDs@TEOS (Fig. S6B and S6D), suggesting that this dual-signal detection showed the favorable anti-interference performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishing the dual-signal assay of tetracycline
ce:section.ce:para: Towards establishing the assay of CDs@TEOS detecting TC, the corresponding standard curve was performed. Both the fluorescence and phosphorescence intensity of CDs@TEOS regularly decreased accompanied by the concentrations of TC rising. Particularly, the linear relationship between the fluorescence of CDs@TEOS and concentrations of TC showed as 2 nM to 90 μM (R2 = 0.993), corresponding to a detection limit of 1.8 nM (Fig. 5B and 5C). For the phosphorescent analysis of TC, the related linear range was obtained from 30 nM to 300 μM (R2 = 0.989), while the detection limit was calculated as 10.3 nM (Fig. 5E and 5F). Thereby, we successfully established a dual-signal method for detecting tetracycline.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of tetracycline in the pork sample
ce:section.ce:para: Considering tetracycline usually serves in animal husbandry, TC was introduced into the pork through the standard addition method to test the practicability of the proposed assay. Accordingly, different concentrations of tetracycline (50, 60 and 70 uM) were introduced into the extract part of pork meat (Table S1), and the recoveries of TC ranged from 96.71 % to 103.72 % by the fluorescence detection with the relative standard deviations of less than 3 %, while the recoveries of TC using the phosphorescence detection were from 98.78 % to 103.11 %. These results suggested that the proposed dual-signal way showed the potential of detecting TC in the real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the fluorescent and phosphorescent response of CDs@TEOS to tetracycline
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the detection mechanism of TC by CDs@TEOS, we designed and performed the further exploration. Towards the interaction between CDs@TEOS and TC, we separately measured the zeta potentials of CDs@TEOS, TC and CDs@TEOS with TC. And the potential of CDs@TEOS increased from −26.7 mV to −20.5 mV when TC was added (Fig. 6 A), proving the interaction between CDs@TEOS and TC occurred. Subsequently, we compared the FTIR spectra of CDs@TEOS without and with TC, and there appeared a wider peak around 3000 cm−1 for CDs@TEOS with TC, which was originated from the interaction between –OH of tetracycline and –NH2 on the surface of CDs@TEOS (Fig. 6B). This finding indicated that and there formed the new substance through CDs@TEOS reacting with TC, which is considered as one critical feature of the static quenching [35,36]. Moreover, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of TC significantly overlapped with both the phosphorescence and fluorescence excitation spectra of CDs@TEOS (Fig. 6C and 6D), demonstrating that the luminescence quenching of CDs@TEOS was also regulated by the internal filtration effect (IFE) [37]. Overall, the static quenching and IFE were both responsible for the quenching of fluorescence and phosphorescence of CDs@TEOS with introducing TC (Fig. 6E).

In brief, we here raised a convenient and economical way for the synthesis of carbon dots with the room-temperature phosphorescence. Specifically, the prepared CDs showed the blue fluorescence under UV, and exhibited the solid-state green phosphorescence with UV off while being coated on the filter paper. Meanwhile, these CDs were suitable to serve as the RTP ink for information encryption based on their advanced optical property. Importantly, we successfully encapsulated the carbon dots by using the matrix of tetraethoxysilane (CDs@TEOS), thus achieving the long-lived room-temperature phosphorescence in aqueous. Subsequently, introducing TC into CDs@TEOS resulted both their fluorescence and phosphorescence decreased, and thus we proposed an innovative dual-signal detection of TC. In addition, we validated the practical value of the current method through the recovery test by using the pork meat supplemented with TC. Again, we also elucidated the detection mechanism as the internal filtration and static quenching towards CDs@TEOS assaying TC.

Jiankang Li: Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Zhiying Feng: Investigation, Methodology, Software. Sen Zhou: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Resources. Linggao Zeng: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Resources. Xiaoming Yang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China (cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0027, CSTB2022NSCQ-MSX1523), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (SWU-XDJH202321), SAMR Science and Technology Program (2022MK105), Chongqing Scientific Institution Incentive Performance Guiding Special Projects (cstc2022jxj120036),Performance Incentive and Guidance Special Project of Scientific Research Organization, Chongqing Science and Technology (cstc2021jxjl130024).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123592.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
